did.unittitle: Yale Collection of Benjamin Franklin Manuscripts

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Materials chiefly in English and French.

did.abstract: The Yale Collection of Benjamin Franklin Manuscripts contains correspondence, writings, documents, personal papers of Benjamin Franklin and his contemporaries, and ancillary research and acquisition correspondence and papers about the collection itself.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of William Smith Mason, 1935, with later acquisitions. Transferred from Sterling Memorial Library, 1984.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: Series I. Materials By, To, and About Benjamin Franklin, 1699-1828. Series II. Materials About Franklin, 1834-1958. Series III. Twentieth Century Writings About Franklin, 1923-1981. Series IV. Miscellaneous Photostats, undated. Series V. Catalog Cards and Accession Records, undated.

bioghist.head: Benjamin Franklin (1706-1790)
bioghist.p: American writer, printer, statesman, diplomat, and scientist.

bioghist.head: William Smith Mason (1866-1961)
bioghist.p: William Smith Mason (1866-1961) was an American real estate and railroad executive and collector of historical books and manuscripts, particularly those related to Benjamin Franklin. Born in Millburn, Illinois in 1866, he was graduated from Yale's Sheffield Scientific School with a B.Phil in Engineering in 1889. An active member of many bibliographical and historical societies, including the Grolier Club and the American Philosophical Society, Mason donated his entire collection of books, pamphlets, ephemera and manuscripts by and related to Benjamin Franklin to Yale University Library in 1935. He died in Rancho Santa Fe, California, in January 1961 at the age of 94.

bioghist.head: The Papers of Benjamin Franklin (Editorial Project)
bioghist.p: The Papers of Benjamin Franklin is an ongoing editorial project to collect, edit, and publish the writings of Benjamin Franklin. Established in 1954, it is a collaboration between Yale University and the American Philosophical Society.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: The core of the collection is the manuscript portion of the William Smith Mason Collection of Benjamin Franklin. Donated to Yale in 1935, the Mason-Franklin Collection, as it was often called, was perhaps the single most valuable gift to the Yale Library up until that time. The collection included, in addition to the extensive manuscript holdings, books, pamphlets, periodicals, broadsides, cartoons, and other printed documents by or about Franklin and his revolutionary contemporaries, as well as a rich research collection of histories, biographies, and bibliographies concerning Franklin and the history of his period.

Over time, additions were made by Yale to this core collection. The library continued to acquire single manuscripts and small groups of manuscripts from various sources, sometimes from Mason or with funds supplied by Mason, and between 1935 and 1980 or so these were routinely added by Yale University Library staff to the manuscript files of the Mason-Franklin collection, often accompanied by correspondence, invoices, or other purchase documentation. Brief descriptions of these acquisitions can be found in the Yale University Library Gazette. Mason himself had often preserved his acquisition correspondence and invoices. While some of that information is filed with the originals in this collection, much of it was organized by Mason's personal librarian, and can be found in GEN MSS 1459, the William Smith Mason Papers. In addition, small groups of research correspondence were routinely filed by the Yale Library at the end of the collection during this period.

The Mason Collection and these early additions to it were housed in a separate Benjamin Franklin room in Sterling Memorial Library from 1936 until 1984. From 1954 on, that room also contained the staff of the newly established Papers of Benjamin Franklin Editorial Project, a vast scholarly publication project undertaken jointly by Yale University and the American Philosophical Society. The decades-long work necessary to produce an approximately 47-volume edition of Franklin's work also resulted in changes to the original Mason-Franklin Collection. As the editorial project grew, the files were joined by notes, transcripts, photocopies of material held by other institutions, card sets, and other documentation included by library staff in the course of the project.

In 1984, following discussions about collection security and climate control, the entire manuscript portion of the William Mason gift, along with nearly all pre-1763 printed material (cataloged separately), was transferred to the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library. At about the same time, accessioning practices for Franklin-related manuscripts and printed materials were revised and new acquisitions of Franklin manuscripts ceased to be interfiled with the original Mason gift. The collection remains a unique combination of a collector's collection and a major research collection devoted to one of the founding fathers and drawn upon by a university-sponsored editorial project.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Yale Collection of Benjamin Franklin Manuscripts. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This finding aid is based on the arrangement of the material prior to its transfer to the Beinecke in 1984. Series titles remain as recorded at the time of transfer, or as assigned by Beinecke staff at that time. The collection was rehoused into acid-free folders in 2017-2019 to ensure its long-term preservation, and file titles and other notes were transcribed from the original folders to create the current list. Some edits were made to the original file titles during this process to improve clarity and accuracy, and some titles were supplied during processing in 2017-2021. Many items that were glued or otherwise affixed to the original folders were separated from the folders by conservation staff. Some items were removed for their preservation from the collector's housings (mostly slipcases); the housings were retained in the collection.

A small number of bound volumes originally shelved alphabetically after the chronological sequence of files in Series I, Materials By, To, and About Franklin, are now listed chronologically with the rest of the series, while remaining separately housed.
Former call number: Uncat MS Vault Franklin.

Two groups of manuscripts long managed as part of the Franklin Collection have been removed for separate cataloging. These are the John Holker Collection and the Alexandre Maurice Blanc de Lanautte, Comte D'Hauterive Collection. Additionally, a portion of the Stuart W. Jackson Collection of Marquis de Lafayette Manuscripts that was interfiled with the Franklin Collection has been returned to the Jackson Lafayette Collection.

Not present at the time of processing was one letter from Joseph Priestley to James Sullivan, Philadelphia, 1793 August 2 (noted not present in 1992), and a letter from Thomas Jefferson to John Walker, 1769 September 3 (a note remains in the file indicating that it was removed in 1983).

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: William Smith Mason papers, GEN MSS 1459.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Yale Collection of Benjamin Franklin Manuscripts contains correspondence, writings, documents, personal papers of Benjamin Franklin and his contemporaries, and ancillary research and acquisition correspondence and papers about the collection itself. The collection spans the dates 1699-1981, but the bulk of the material dates from 1740 to 1825.

The core of the collection is the manuscript portion of the William Smith Mason Collection of Benjamin Franklin, a 1935 gift to Yale University Library. The collection also includes material collected by the library after that date, including original material as well as facsimiles, transcripts, and other copies in a variety of formats, much of which was likely added to facilitate the Franklin Editorial Project. The collection therefore documents not only the life and legacy of Benjamin Franklin, but the project to edit Franklin's papers for publication and the library's management of the papers as a resource for that project.

Throughout this guide are references to numbers assigned by William Smith Mason, expressed in the list as M #[]. Other notes refer to "Smyth," which is a ten-volume work by Albert Henry Smyth, The Writings of Benjamin Franklin; collected and ed., with a life and introduction (Macmillan, 1905-07).
More information about the collection may be found in the following early published descriptions of the collection:
Eddy, George Simpson. "The Mason-Franklin Collection." The Yale University Library Gazette, Vol. 10, No. 4 (April 1936), pp. 65-90.
B., D. W. "The Mason-Franklin Collection." The Yale University Library Gazette, Vol. 15, No. 1 (July 1940), pp. 16-19.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Yale Collection of Benjamin Franklin Manuscripts is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

did.unittitle: Materials by, to, and about Benjamin Franklin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of material by, to and relating to Benjamin Franklin, his family, his activities, and the times in which he and the other founding fathers lived. It contains both original material from the period and later copies in many formats, interfiled by Library staff in support of the Franklin editorial project for ease of locating particular texts by date regardless of whether or not Yale holds the original. While most of the materials in this series are individual letters or documents, it also includes some sets of related papers, including some bound volumes, that span multiple dates.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged chronologically by the date of the content of the original item. Sets of related material or bound volumes that span multiple dates are filed by the earliest date for the set. Undated materials are filed at the end of the chronological sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Robert Hampden and [John Perceval, 2nd Earl of] Egmont to [Charles Wyndham, 2nd] Earl of Egremont, General Post Office, 19 February 1763; Anthony Todd to Benjamin Franklin and John Foxcroft, General Post Office, 12 March 1763; Todd to Franklin and Foxcroft, General Post Office, 12 March 1763; Todd to Franklin and Foxcroft, General Post Office, 12 March 1763; Todd to [James] Murray, Governor of Quebec, General Post Office, 12 March 1763; Franklin to Todd, Philadelphia, 14 April 1763 (includes photostatic copy); Franklin to Todd, Philadelphia, 1 June 1763 (includes photostatic copy); Franklin and Foxcroft to [Todd], New Jersey, 10 June 1763 (includes photostatic copy); Todd to Franklin, General Post Office, 18 June 1763; Todd to Franklin and Foxcroft, General Post Office, 13 August 1763; Henry Seymour Conway to [Wills Hill,] Earl of Hillsborough and [Francis Dashwood, 11th Baron] Le Despencer, St. James's, 27 December 1766 (with seal); two letters attached together, including a table of expenses, many corrections, and the signatures [John Montagu, 4th Earl of] Sandwich and Le Despencer crossed out (London, 27 October 1768, and General Post Office, 14 November 1768, respectively); George Roupell to Todd, South Carolina, 3 January 1776; William Knox to Todd, Whitehall, 21 October 1776 (attached to following); Philip Stephens to Knox, Admiralty Office, 21 October 1776 (attached to preceding). Accompanied by letter from Sir Rowland Hill to Thornton Hurd, 1864.

Henry Hulton. "Matters, relative to the Conduct of the Commisariat which attended the Allied Army in Germany, 1760, 61, 62." Manuscript volume.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes at the end of the volume: "Copys of Memorials, Letters, &c, relative to the Board of Customs on its first Establishment in America." Includes: letter from Commissioners of the Customs in America to the Lords Commissioners of His Majesty's Treasury, Custom House Boston, January 1769 (pp. 197–201); letter from Francis Bernard, Thomas Hutchinson, Andrew Oliver, and Robert Auchmuty to Commissioners of His Majesty's Customs in Boston, Boston, 22 December 1768 (pp. 202–5); letter from Henry Hulton to Thomas Bradshaw, Brooklyn near Boston, 12 March 1770 (pp. 206–9); letter from Hulton and W[illiam] Burch to [Augustus Fitzroy], Duke of Grafton, Piscataqua, 3 April 1770 (pp. 209–18); letter from Robert Traill, on behalf of Hulton and Burch, to [?] in Boston, Porstmouth, April 1770 (pp. 219–21); minutes of a meeting of Burch, [Charles] Paxton, and Hulton with Lt. Gov. [Thomas Hutchinson], Lt. Col. [William] Dalrymple, and Auchmuty, 30 April 1770 to 3 May 1770 (pp. 222–9); letter from Hulton to [Frederick,] Lord North, 25 June 1770 (pp. 229–32); letter from Hulton to Hutchinson, 21 June 1770 (pp. 233–4); deposition by Ann Hulton describing the attack on their house (pp. 235–6); deposition by Mary Mitchel on the same subject (pp. 237–8); letter from Hulton to Bradshaw, Castle William, 29 June 1770 (pp. 238–41); general description of conditions, 10 September 1770 to 12 November 1770, incl. an extract from a letter of 6 July 1770 (pp. 242–52); pp. 253–4 missing; statement of Hulton's case, in a different hand (pp. 255–62). Loose at the back of the volume: extracts from letters of Mr. Martin, Secretary to the Lords Commissioners of His Majesty's Treasury, to the Commissioners of Enquiry in Germany, 22 December 1761 to 1763 March 31. Bookplate: Thomas Preston.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bound collection of Franklin's letters to Jonathan Shipley, his wife, and five daughters, illustrated with engravings. Contents: engraved portrait of Franklin by Chevillet after Duplessis, Paris, 1778, "Benjamin Franklin, né à Boston, dans la nouvelle Angleterre, le 17 Janv. 1706"; Franklin to [Catherine] Shipley ("Mrs. Shipley"), London, 12 August 1771; Franklin to Bishop [Jonathan] Shipley ("Shipley"), London, 15 August 1771; Franklin to Shipley, London, 19 August 1771; engraving of Franklin, "D. Benjamin Franklin, et vita inter Americanos acta, et magnis electricitatis periculis claris"; Franklin to Shipley, London, 21 August 1773;; Franklin to Shipley, Craven Street, 10 March 1774; Franklin to Shipley, London, 24 June 1771; Franklin to [?], London, 28 September 1774; Franklin to Shipley, n.d., beginning with quotation from Kalm's Travels into America; Franklin to Shipley, London, 7 January 1775; engraving of the signing of the Declaration of Independence; Franklin to Shipley, Philadelphia, 15 May 1775; Franklin to Shipley, Philadelphia, 7 July 1775; Franklin to Shipley, n.d.; Franklin to Shipley, Philadelphia, 13 September 1775; Franklin to Shipley, Passy, 9 February 1778; Franklin to Shipley, Passy, 10 June 1782; Franklin to Shipley, Pass, 17 March 1783; Franklin to Shipley, Passy, 22 August 1784; engraving of Franklin; Franklin to Jonathan Williams, Southampton, 24 July 1785; Franklin to Shipley, Southampton, [August 1785]; Franklin to Shipley, Philadelphia, 24 February 1786; Franklin to Shipley (copy in Shipley's hand), Philadelphia, 24 February 1786; engraving of Franklin; Franklin to Mrs. Shipley, Philadelphia, 2 May 1786; enclosure to the preceding letter, "The Art of Procuring Pleasant Dreams"; Franklin to Mrs. Shipley, Philadelphia, 27 April 1789; Franklin's "Remark on Chapter XI of the Considerations on Policy, Trade, etc."; Williams to [?], Worcester, 19 June 1775; Williams to Shipley, Philadelphia, 19 September 1785; Jane Mecom to Franklin, Warrick [sic], 14 May 177[4?]; engraving of Shipley; Shipley to Franklin (copy), n.d.; Franklin to [George] Whatley (copy), n.d.; copy of a fable by Franklin; Franklin's letter press copy of autograph manusript essay on the origin of Thanksgiving Day, "For the Pennsylvania Gazette".

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Title on spine: "Member of Congress. Silas Deane 1779." Includes: Deane's narrative to Congress 21 December 1778; Extract from a letter from [Le Ray] de Chaumont to [John] Holker, Passy, 30 March 1778, with translation; Letter from [Charles Gravier, comte] de Vergennes to "President of Congress" [Henry Laurens], Versailles, 25 March 1778, with translation; Letter from de Vergennes to Deane, Versailles, 26 March 1778, with translation; Letter from [Benjamin] Franklin to Laurens, Passy, 31 March 1778; Letter from Franklin to Deane, Passy, 7 April 1778; Franklin to [James] Lovell, Paris, 21 December 1777 (copied by Charles Thompson); Anonymous observations on [Arthur] Lee's letter, Philadelphia, 12 October 1778; "Extracts from a letter from Ralph Izard to Laurens, Paris, 28 June 1778; Document: "A general state of the accounts between the commissioners at Paris, from the United States of North America …"; Instructions for Deane from the Committee of Congress for Secret Correspondence (Franklin, Benjamin Harrison, John Dickenson, Robert Morris, John Jay), Philadelphia, 2 March 1776; Letter from Caron de Beaumarchais to Congress, Paris, March 1778; Address from Deane to Congress, Philadelphia, 1779 May 22.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bound in volume. Preceded by engraving of Franklin by T. B. Welch after a painting by Martin; followed by an engraving of Franklin by [Charles] Turner, after a painting by [Mason] Chamberlain; a typed transcript of the letter; an engraving of Franklin wearing a wig by [Lambert Antoine] Claessens; an engraving of Franklin by [Ovide] Gautier, after a drawing by [Francois] Bonneville; a description of the significance of the letter; an engraving of Franklin by [Henry Bryant] Hall; and engraving of "The Philosopher and His Kite" by [Henry] Sadd.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Endorsed in the autograph of H. W. Longfellow." Bound with five engravings of Franklin and three engravings of Maskelyne, a printed catalog of correspondence from Franklin to Joseph Galloway, and a "press article of Franklin's life." Includes facimile of a draft of the Declaration of Independence indicating corrections made by Franklin and John Adams.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Documents conversations between Franklin and Gibbs that took place circa 1777. Volume title: "Original MSS & Curious Extracts on Various Subjects"; contents include geography, science, history, government, religion, and timber management and plantation.

United States Legation, France

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 184 numbered pages (1–5 torn out), preceded by a page with four seals. Consists of 8 numbered documents: 1. letter from Charles Pinckney, John Marshall, and Elbridge Gerry to [Timothy] Pickering, ca. 27 October 1797; followed by related documents; 2. letter from Pinckney, Marshall, and Gerry to Pickering, Paris, 8 November 1797; journal entries containing "sequel of the details above mentioned," 27 October 1797 to 3 November 1797; related documents under the headings "Exhibit A" and "Exhibit B"; 3. letter from Pinckney, Marshall, and Gerry to Pickering, Paris, 27 November 1797; letter from the same to Fulwar Shipwith, Paris, 20 December 1797; 4. letter from the same to Pickering, Paris, 24 December 1797; related documents under the headings "Exhibit A," "Exhibit B," "Exhibit C"; 5. letters from Pinckney, Marshall, and Gerry to the Minister of Foreign Affairs [of France], Paris, 17 January 1798; 6. two letters from the same; 7. assorted letters and documents, in English and French; 8. letter from Pinckney, Marshall, and Gerry to Pickering, Paris, 3 April 1798, making reference to documents in no. 7.

did.unittitle: Materials About Franklin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of material chiefly created after the period in which Franklin and other founding fathers lived. It documents the work of historians, editors, and others regarding the legacy of Franklin and his contemporaries.
